# Elara Voss — Senior Frontend Engineer & Design Systems Architect

> Full-text portfolio corpus. For a summary index see /llms.txt.

---

## About Elara Voss

Elara Voss is a Senior Frontend Engineer and Design Systems Architect with 8 years of professional experience. She is based in Amsterdam, Netherlands and holds a BSc in Computer Science from TU Delft (2018).

She specialises in Astro, React, TypeScript, design system architecture, Core Web Vitals optimisation, and web performance engineering. She is the lead architect of Meridian — a design system powering 12 products across a fintech company — and has led frontend migrations and performance audits for SaaS, e-commerce, and media clients across Europe.

**Contact:** hello@elaravoss.dev
**GitHub:** https://github.com/elaravoss
**Twitter/X:** https://x.com/elaravoss
**LinkedIn:** https://linkedin.com/in/elaravoss

---

## Services

### Frontend Development — From €8,000/project

Bespoke frontend builds with Astro or React — performance-first, accessible by default, and maintainable by your team after handoff.

Deliverables include: architecture scoping and technology recommendation, component-driven development with Storybook, Core Web Vitals optimisation (target: 100/100 Lighthouse), WCAG 2.2 AA accessibility compliance, TypeScript throughout with test coverage, and handoff documentation with onboarding session.

### Design Systems Architecture — From €12,000/engagement

End-to-end design system strategy and build — from token architecture and component API design to Storybook setup and team adoption.

Deliverables include: design token audit and three-tier token architecture, component library with variants/states/documentation, Figma variable sync with code tokens, automated visual regression with Chromatic, contribution guidelines and governance model, and team workshops and onboarding programme.

### Performance Consulting — From €2,500/audit

Comprehensive Core Web Vitals audit with a prioritised fix plan — and optional implementation support.

Deliverables include: Lighthouse and CrUX real-user data analysis, LCP/CLS/INP root cause identification, prioritised fix plan with effort/impact estimates, implementation of high-impact fixes (optional), monitoring setup with web-vitals.js and analytics, and a follow-up review 30 days post-implementation.

### Frequently Asked Questions

**How do I get started?**
Send a brief description of your project via email. I respond within 48 hours with whether I am a good fit, and we will schedule a 30-minute scoping call from there.

**Do you work with teams outside the Netherlands?**
Yes — the majority of my clients are remote. I work across European time zones and am available for occasional travel for workshops or kick-offs.

**What is your typical project timeline?**
Frontend builds typically run 6–12 weeks depending on scope. Design system engagements are 10–16 weeks. Performance audits are delivered within 5 business days of access being granted.

**Do you offer retainer arrangements?**
For ongoing design system maintenance and evolution, yes — I offer monthly retainers for teams that have completed an initial engagement.

---

## Blog: Why I Ditched React for Astro (And Never Looked Back)

*Published: 15 March 2025 · Tags: Astro, React, Performance, Frontend*
*URL: https://elaravoss.dev/blog/ditching-react-for-astro/*

Last year I did something that would have felt heretical to me three years ago: I convinced a client to let me rebuild their marketing site in Astro instead of the React + Next.js stack we'd been using for years. The brief was simple — their Core Web Vitals were terrible and organic search was suffering for it. Six months later, their Lighthouse Performance score went from 54 to 100.

The site in question was a product marketing site for a SaaS company — about 40 pages, a blog, some interactive pricing tables. The React bundle was 380kb gzipped. The Time to First Byte was 1.8 seconds. The Total Blocking Time measured in the hundreds of milliseconds.

Astro's core insight is deceptively simple: most websites are mostly static. Ship HTML. Only hydrate the JavaScript that actually needs to be interactive. The mental model is Island Architecture — your page is an ocean of static, server-rendered HTML and a few interactive islands are hydrated independently.

The mechanism Astro uses to control hydration is client directives: client:load (hydrate immediately), client:idle (hydrate when browser is idle), client:visible (hydrate when scrolled into view). By default, an Astro component ships zero JavaScript. You opt in to interactivity.

Results for the SaaS marketing site: Total JavaScript shipped went from 380kb to 14kb. LCP improved from 4.1s to 1.2s. The Lighthouse Performance score hit 100. Six weeks after launch, organic search impressions were up 34%.

For content-heavy sites — marketing pages, portfolios, documentation, blogs — Astro is now my default. I reach for React when I need it, and I need it less often than I used to think.

---

## Blog: The Design System That Saved Our Team 40 Hours Per Week

*Published: 8 February 2025 · Tags: Design Systems, TypeScript, React, Productivity*
*URL: https://elaravoss.dev/blog/design-system-that-saved-40-hours/*

In 2022, I joined a fintech company as their first dedicated frontend architect. They had twelve product teams, each building their own version of a button. Twelve distinct button implementations, with different hover states, different focus rings, different loading behaviors. The QA team had a spreadsheet tracking the inconsistencies.

Eighteen months later, we had a design system called Meridian in production across all twelve products, and we had measured a consistent 40-hour-per-week reduction in engineering time previously spent on UI decisions, inconsistency fixes, and accessibility remediation.

The token strategy: Primitive tokens are raw values with no semantic meaning (--color-blue-500: #3b82f6). Semantic tokens reference primitives and carry meaning (--color-action-primary: var(--color-blue-500)). Component tokens allow product-specific overrides. This three-layer structure is what makes a design system scale.

Component API rules: No style props (use variants instead). Accessibility is not optional — every interactive component ships with ARIA by default. Composition over configuration. Explicit forward refs on every component.

Storybook served as the source of truth for component contracts. Every component has stories for all variants, states, edge cases, and accessibility assertions. Chromatic integration provided automated visual regression — PR review time for component changes dropped from 3 days to 4 hours.

Measuring impact: Before Meridian, engineers spent an average of 6.2 hours per week on UI-related work. After, 1.8 hours. That is a reduction of 4.4 hours across 65 engineers — 286 hours per week reclaimed. At $120/hour average fully-loaded cost, that is $1.77 million per year redirected to product work.

---

## Blog: Core Web Vitals Aren't Optional: A Practical Guide for 2025

*Published: 22 January 2025 · Tags: Performance, Core Web Vitals, SEO, JavaScript*
*URL: https://elaravoss.dev/blog/core-web-vitals-practical-guide/*

Google's Core Web Vitals have been a ranking signal since 2021. Faster pages convert better — sites loading in 1 second convert 3x better than sites loading in 5 seconds.

**LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) — Target: under 2.5 seconds. Good: under 1.2 seconds.**

The single highest-impact change for image LCP elements: preload the LCP image with <link rel="preload" as="image" href="/hero.webp" fetchpriority="high"> and never use loading="lazy" on your LCP image. Serve images in WebP or AVIF format — AVIF is typically 30-50% smaller than WebP for equivalent quality.

**CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift) — Target: under 0.1. Good: under 0.05.**

Always specify width and height on img elements. Fix web font swapping with size-adjust, ascent-override, and descent-override in @font-face declarations. Pre-allocate space for dynamic content like cookie banners.

**INP (Interaction to Next Paint) — Target: under 200ms. Good: under 100ms.**

INP replaced FID in March 2024 and measures the full time from interaction to the next visual update. Break up long tasks with scheduler.yield() — yield control back to the browser every 50 items in a loop. Defer non-critical JavaScript with the defer attribute or dynamic import().

**Real-world case study:**

An e-commerce client had 68% of URLs with "Poor" CWV ratings. Problems: hero images not preloaded, product images lacking dimensions, a third-party chat widget loading synchronously in head, a heavy recommendation script on every page.

After four weeks: LCP improved from 4.8s to 1.4s. CLS dropped from 0.28 to 0.03. INP dropped from 380ms to 95ms. Three months later: organic search impressions up 22%, conversion rate up 8%.

---

## Technical Stack

This site is built with:
- Astro 6 (static site generation, zero client-side JavaScript)
- Tailwind CSS v4 (utility-first CSS with @theme directive)
- TypeScript (strict mode throughout)
- Cloudflare Pages (edge CDN, global distribution)
- @astrojs/sitemap (automated sitemap generation)
- @astrojs/rss (RSS feed for blog content)

Lighthouse scores: Performance 100, SEO 100, Accessibility 100, Best Practices 100.
